description: Inside a coffee shop, Chandler stands facing Rachel. "Told this guy that I was looking for a fling. You don't tell the guy that. Why not?" he says. Rachel listens with her hands on her hips.
Chandler points his index finger. "I'd be thrilled if I heard that some hot girl was just looking to get oh, I see," his volume dropping on the final words as he lowers his hands.
Rachel looks down at the floor, then back up at Chandler.

Rachel waves her hands as she speaks. "Between you telling him I wanted to have a fling and me putting out on the first date oh, he's sure to get the wrong idea." She drops her arms and slumps back onto the sofa.
The setting changes to a restaurant kitchen. Joey, wearing a white shirt and red tie, uses a fork to eat from a clear plastic container. He turns.
Monica, in a white chef's coat, walks past him. "Hey, Joey, could you pass the cheeks?"
Joey replies, "Yeah," holding up the container.
Joey turns and follows Monica.
Joey hands the container to Monica as they walk. "Listen, I prefer it if you didn't call me Joey. Because since I don't know anyone here I thought it might be fun to try out a cool work"
"nickname. Hey, Dragon."
Stu, wearing a white shirt and a bright green tie, approaches Joey.
Stu hands two envelopes to Joey. "Here's your tips for Monday and Tuesday."
Joey accepts the envelopes.
Joey opens an envelope and looks at the cash. "Looks like there's, like, $300 in this one."
Stu nods. "Yeah, people get pretty generous around the holidays. And it never hurts to wear tight trousers," pointing at Joey's pants.
Joey nods, looking down.
Joey says, "Okay,"
Monica claps her hands once and speaks loudly across the kitchen.
"could the waiters gather around to hear tonight's specials?" The waiters approach a metal table.
Monica reads from a notepad. "Okay. First, there's a Chilean sea bass prepared with a mango relish on a bed."
She looks up. "Why is nobody writing these down?" Stu begins, "Because"
"we can remember them. And because you're all going to make up fake specials and make me cook them like you did the other night." Monica responds.
Stu gestures with his hand.
Stu smiles slightly. "Well, sure, that too."
Monica stares at Stu, then lowers her gaze.
Monica waves her hand. "Okay, forget the specials for a minute."
Monica shifts her weight.
She looks at the group. "Here's the thing."
She looks down again.
"For the last two weeks, I have"
Monica keeps her eyes downcast.
Monica looks up. "tried really hard to create a positive atmosphere." Stu cups a hand to his mouth and says, "Can't hear you."
Monica glares at Stu. Audience laughter is audible.
Monica raises her volume. "Positive atmosphere."
She holds her hand up at eye level. "But I have had it up to here."
The waiters, including Stu and Joey, stand silently and look at her.
Monica gestures outward. "From now on, it is going to be my way or the highway."
She pauses. "All right?"
Monica looks around the room.
Monica puts her hands on her hips. "Does anybody have a problem with that?"
Stu and Joey stand with their arms crossed or holding money, maintaining eye contact in silence.
Monica's gaze fixes on Joey.
"Hey, new guy."
Monica takes a small step toward him.
"I said, does anybody have a problem with that?" she asks, leaning slightly forward.
Joey looks at her.
Joey answers, "No, ma'am."
Monica stares at him, her mouth slightly parted.
Stu places a hand on Joey's back. "Hey, he has a name. It's Dragon."
Stu turns his head.
Stu looks at Monica. "You want to know your name? Check your hat."
Stu looks at Joey and smiles. "You get the hat, right?" Joey nods.
Monica looks from Stu to Joey.
Monica turns fully to Joey. "What the hell just happened?"
Joey steps forward.
Joey raises his hands defensively. "I am so, so sorry. I was going to do it."
Joey moves his arms.
Joey adds, "Really?"
Joey gestures rapidly with both hands holding cash. "But then I was standing there with $327 in one hand and $238 in the other hand and I was thinking,"
Joey looks upward. "wow, it's been a long time since I had"
Joey lowers his head.
"327 plus $238," he finishes, shaking his fists slightly. Audience laughter is heard.
Monica moves closer to him.
Monica gestures sharply. "Joey, we had a deal. That's why you're here. I've got to fire you."
"And I got to pay rent," Joey responds, his voice rising.
Joey points a finger.
"Look, how about this? You don't fire me."
Joey gestures toward the other side of the kitchen. "Instead, I stay here, I gain their trust"
"and then they'll start listening to all the nice things I've been saying about you."
Monica looks at him steadily.
"Kind of things you've been saying?" she asks.
"Well, nothing yet. They really hate you, and I want to fit in," Joey says, shifting his stance.
The setting changes back to the coffee shop. Phoebe sits holding an acoustic guitar.
Phoebe sings, "Happy. Happy hanukkah, Chandler and Monica."
Phoebe strums the guitar.
"Merry. Merry," she sings.
Chandler sits on the sofa. "You know what, Phoebes?"
Phoebe stops playing. "What?" Chandler answers, "I'm not Jewish."
Phoebe looks at him. Audience laughter is audible.
In the coffee shop, Ross sleeps on the couch next to Chandler. Audience laughter is audible.
Phoebe plays her guitar and sings, "Ross doesn't really decorate his tree with floss but you don't hear him complaining, do you?" Ross remains asleep, his mouth open.
Ross jerks awake, closing his mouth and shifting upright. Chandler looks at him.
"Bad dream?" Chandler asks.
"I wasn't sleeping," Ross replies, adjusting his jacket.
"Oh, yeah? Then what was Phoebe's song about?" Chandler asks, gesturing toward Phoebe with his thumb.
Ross looks upward momentarily, then says, "The One with the Cat."
Phoebe nods. Ross stands up and grabs his leather jacket.
Ross says, "I gotta go. I got another date." Chandler asks, "Oh. So did you pick one yet?"
Ross puts his arms into the jacket sleeves, replying, "No. It turns out the one from uptown"
"was making a joke. But it was a different joke than I thought. It wasn't that funny," Ross explains, keeping his gaze forward.
"So I'm still looking," Ross says. Phoebe interrupts, pointing her hand toward him.
"You know, you don't really like the one from uptown and you're too exhausted from dating the one in" Phoebe continues.
"Poughkeepsie. So I say you just end them both. Okay?" Phoebe adds, gesturing with an open palm.
"You take a train up to Poughkeepsie and you break up with her. And on" she says, making a sweeping arm motion.
"your way back, you break up with uptown. And then by the time you get home tonight, you're done," Phoebe finishes, resting her hands on her lap.
Ross nods slowly. "You know what? You're right. Thank you."
Phoebe adds, "I had a similar problem when I lived in Prague."
Chandler's brow furrows as he looks at Phoebe.
"Prague?" Chandler asks. Phoebe strums her guitar and replies, "So much you don't know."
Phoebe continues playing her guitar while Ross and Chandler sit quietly.
The setting changes to the exterior of an apartment building at night. Inside an apartment, Chandler swings a miniature golf club on the carpet.
Rachel enters through the front door. Chandler hits a small ball with the club. Rachel stands near the kitchen counter with her hands resting on her hips.
"Chandler, Patrick just ended things with me," Rachel says, walking toward him.
"Did you or did you not tell him that I was looking for a serious" Rachel asks, extending one hand toward him.
"relationship?" she finishes.
Chandler claps his hands together. "I did. I absolutely did." Rachel says, "You idiot."
"I'm sure you're right. But why?" Chandler asks, holding his arms out.
Rachel steps closer, raising both hands near her chest.
"You don't tell a guy that you're looking for a serious relationship," Rachel says, waving her hands.
"You don't tell the guy that now you scared him away," Rachel adds, tapping her chest with her fingers.
Chandler's mouth pulls into a tight line. "Oh, man. I am sorry. I am so sorry," he says softly.
"You know, you should never be allowed to talk to people," Rachel says, pointing a finger directly at him.
Chandler raises his hands, palms facing forward, his mouth open.
"I know," Chandler agrees.
Rachel turns and walks away from him.
"Now I'm right back where he started. Oh, this sucks," Rachel groans, walking toward a recliner chair.
"Being alone sucks," she adds, falling backward into the chair.
Chandler turns toward her, extending one hand.
"You know what?" Chandler says.
"You're going to meet somebody," he says.
"You're a great catch," he continues, stepping closer to her chair.
"You know, when I was telling all those guys about you I didn't have to lie once," Chandler says, sitting on the chair's armrest and placing his arm behind her.
Rachel tilts her head to look up at him.
"Really?" Rachel asks, her volume softer than her previous lines.
"Yeah," Chandler replies, stroking her hair.
"You graduated magna cum laude, right?" Chandler asks.
Rachel stares at him, her facial expression becoming still.
"No, it doesn't matter," Chandler says, pulling his hand away quickly.
Rachel closes her eyes and rests her head against his side. Chandler resumes stroking her hair.
"Hey, you know what?" Chandler asks.
"I got two tickets to tonight's Ranger game. You want to come with me?" he asks, pointing at his chest.
"Cute guys in little shorts? Sure," Rachel replies, looking up at him.
Chandler pauses, looking at her.
"Well, actually, it's a hockey team," he clarifies.
"So it's angry Canadians with no teeth," he adds with a slight smile.
"Well, that sounds fun too," Rachel says, snuggling her head back onto his shoulder.
"Thank you. Okay," Chandler says.
Chandler hugs her closer with both arms. They sit quietly.
"Have you ever been with a woman?" Chandler asks, staring straight ahead.
Rachel pulls her head up sharply and turns to stare at him.
"What?" Rachel asks, her voice rising in volume.
"Chandler, what is the matter with you?" she asks loudly. "So there is no good time to ask that question," Chandler replies, gesturing outward with one hand.
The setting changes to a moving passenger train exterior at night, then shifts to the dimly lit interior. A train conductor walks down the aisle.
"Station is poughkeepsie," the conductor announces.
"Poughkeepsie," the conductor repeats, walking past the seated passengers.
Ross is asleep, leaning his head against the window. Outside on the platform, a woman in a green hat walks up to the glass.
"Ross," the woman says, knocking on the window.
"Ross," she repeats.
She points at him through the glass. Ross remains asleep.
"Ross," she yells.
The woman waves her hand frantically near the glass.
"Ross," she yells.
"Ross," she shouts, hitting the window pane.
"Ross. Ross," she continues yelling as the train slowly begins to move forward.
Ross stirs, shifts his head, and smiles while his eyes remain closed. The setting changes to a restaurant kitchen.
A female chef, Monica, slides food onto a plate and says, "I need more swordfish."
"Can you give me some more swordfish?" Monica asks a passing waiter. "I don't speak English," the waiter replies while walking away.
Monica stares after the waiter, her jaw visibly tightening.
In a bustling restaurant kitchen, the Kitchen Worker picks apart parsley at a stainless steel counter.
The Kitchen Worker looks off-screen and says, "You did a minute ago. Well, I don't know what to tell you."
Monica, carrying a stack of plates, sets them down on a nearby counter and turns.
Monica says, "Fine," her mouth pressed closed afterward.
Monica walks past the counters and enters the walk-in refrigerator, pulling the door shut behind her. Stu approaches the refrigerator door and pushes it fully closed.
Stu gestures to another worker. Together, they push a metal prep table laden with food directly in front of the refrigerator door, blocking it.
Other kitchen staff gather around the table, watching the refrigerator door. Stu smiles with his lips pressed together. Audience laughter is audible.
Monica's muffled voice calls from inside the refrigerator, "Somebody let me out," as loud banging sounds on the door.
Banging on the door continues as Monica says, "Please. Come on, I'm cold," her voice muffled.
The kitchen staff around the table laugh, blending with audible audience laughter. Stu's hands rest on the prep table.
Monica continues, her voice still muffled, "covered in marinara sauce. Let me out."
The staff look toward a second door. The door swings open, revealing Monica, the front of her white chef's coat stained heavily with red sauce. Audience laughter plays.
Stu looks at Monica and asks, "You found that handle, did you?" Audience laughter follows.
Monica stares at Stu, her mouth pressed into a thin line.
Monica states, "Not funny." The Kitchen Worker replies, "Well, that's not true."
Monica turns her head to look at the Kitchen Worker, then addresses the whole room, extending her arms.
"I'm a good person, and I'm a good chef," Monica says, stepping forward.
Gesturing to her stained coat, she continues, "And I don't deserve to have marinara sauce all over me. You know what?"
Monica lowers her arms.
Monica says, "If you want me to quit this job and all you have to do—" her arms slightly raised.
Joey, standing among the staff, steps forward and loudly interrupts, "hey, Chef Geller. And that little speech you made the other day?"
Joey points to himself and says, "Well, I got a problem with it."
Monica stares at Joey, her brow furrowed.
Monica asks, "You do?" Joey replies, "You bet I do," standing with his hands on his hips.
Joey shifts his weight and gestures with one hand.
"I just wasn't listening then, that's all," Joey states, followed by audience laughter.
Monica looks at him, tilting her head slightly.
Monica steps toward Joey, her volume rising, "If you want a problem, I'll give you a problem."
Joey gestures outward and asks, "What are you going to do here? Are you going to fire me?"
Monica stares at him fixedly.
Monica shouts, "You bet your ass I'm going to fire you. Now get out of my kitchen." Pointing to the door, she yells, "Get out."
Joey turns and walks out of the kitchen. The remaining staff stand in silence, watching the door.
Monica turns back to the staff and says, "All right."
Monica surveys the room, her posture straight.
"Anybody else got a problem?" she asks, her hands on her hips.
Monica turns her gaze toward the Kitchen Worker and asks, "How about you, chuckles?"
Monica shifts her gaze to Stu.
Monica stares at Stu and asks, "You think this is funny now?"
Stu shakes his head slightly.
Stu replies softly, "No."

Monica raises her arms and dances, wiggling her body. "How about if I dance around all covered in sauce?" she asks loudly as audience laughter plays. She stops dancing and says, "You think it's funny now?"
Stu shakes his head again.
Stu says, "No, it's really good," prompting audience laughter.
Monica looks at him, her lips slightly parted.
Monica nods once and says, "Good," her voice steady.
Pointing to different staff members, Monica orders loudly, "Now take those salads to table four and you get the swordfish and you get a haircut?" The staff members quickly move to comply.
The setting changes to the exterior of a moving train at night. The view then shifts inside to a dimly lit passenger train car.
A conductor walks down the aisle holding a clipboard and announces, "Last stop, Montreal."
The conductor continues walking away and adds, "The stop is Montreal," his voice fading.
Ross, asleep with his head leaning against a window, opens his eyes and sits up quickly.
Looking around, Ross says, "What?"
The woman sitting next to Ross turns to him and smiles, her gaze steady.
The woman says, "I made a bet with myself that you had beautiful eyes."
She smiles wider and adds, "Now that I see them, I win."
Ross blinks, pulling his jacket tighter around his chest.
Ross asks, "What?"
The woman keeps her gaze on Ross, still smiling.
The woman says, "We're at my stop," then pauses briefly before asking, "But would you like to have coffee?"
Ross turns to look out the dark window, then looks back at the woman.
Ross asks, "Are we really in Montreal?" his brow furrowed.
The woman nods and replies, "Yes, we are. So, coffee?"
Ross looks around the train car briefly.
Ross looks back at the woman and says, "Coffee sounds great."
The woman stands up, gathering her coat from the seat.
Ross stands up to face her and asks, "Wait, so you live in Montreal?"
The woman looks up at him and replies, "Oh, no, but it's just a two hour ferry ride to Nova Scotia." Audience laughter is audible.
Ross's jaw drops slightly, and his eyes widen. The setting changes back to the restaurant kitchen, where Monica stirs a pot on the stove.
Joey enters the kitchen wearing a dark coat and tie. Looking toward the staff, he says loudly, "Well, I guess I should have thought about my wife and kids before I talked back to Chef Geller."
Monica turns her head to look at Joey.

Monica softly says, "Thanks." Joey turns back toward the staff, grasping his coat lapels, and says loudly, "Yep. Looks like it's going to be a lean Christmas at the Dragon house this year." Audience laughter plays.
Joey presses his lips together as he looks at Monica. Monica smiles, the corners of her eyes crinkling.
Monica suddenly drops her smile, looks at the staff, and says sharply, "Enough."
The staff members quickly turn away and resume their work.
In a coffee shop decorated with a Christmas tree, string lights, and garlands, Phoebe stands at a microphone holding an acoustic guitar. Several people sit on couches and chairs facing her.
An uncertain speaker says, "Lean, lean, lean." Phoebe remains at the microphone.
Phoebe adjusts her fingers on the guitar fretboard and smiles toward the audience.
Phoebe says, "So this is a very special holiday song..."
Pointing a finger toward the audience, Phoebe continues, "...that I wrote for some very important people to me."
Joey and Ross sit at a small table; Joey smiles and turns to Ross, who smiles back. On a nearby couch, Chandler, Rachel, and Monica sit together.
Phoebe begins strumming the guitar and sings, "Went to the store, sat on Santa's lap..."
Phoebe continues singing, "...asked him to bring my friends all kinds of crap." Audience laughter is audible.
Chandler rests his arm on the back of the couch behind Rachel as they watch Phoebe.
Phoebe sings, "Then all you need is to write them a song."
She continues singing, "They haven't heard it yet, so don't try to sing along." Audience laughter plays.
Phoebe stops strumming and looks directly at the audience.
Speaking into the microphone with a flat pitch, Phoebe says, "Don't. Sing along." Audience laughter plays.
Joey and Ross look toward Phoebe, their smiles fading.
Phoebe resumes strumming and sings, "Monica, Monica, have a happy Hanukkah."
Monica smiles broadly, her teeth showing. Audience laughter plays.
Phoebe sings, "Saw Santa Claus. He said hello to Ross." Audience laughter plays.
Joey smiles and pats Ross's arm. Ross raises his eyebrows and smiles, his arms crossed.
Phoebe sings, "And please tell Joey..." as Joey watches her.
Joey nods repeatedly as Phoebe sings, "...Christmas will be snowing." Audience laughter plays.
Joey stops nodding, his eyebrows drawing together, and he turns to Ross, who looks back at him. Audience laughter peaks.
Phoebe sings, "And Rachel and Chandler..."
Slowing her pace, Phoebe sings, "...how do? Lemon blood," and stops strumming. Audience laughter plays.
Chandler and Rachel turn to look at each other with furrowed brows.
Phoebe smiles and speaks into the microphone, "Happy holidays, everybody."
The audience claps. Phoebe waves her hand and smiles.
The screen fades to black, and the audio goes silent.
A studio production logo and copyright text appear on the black screen.
